title: Dhammapada
subtitle: The Path of the Dhamma
description: A collection of sayings of the Buddha in verse form, one of the most widely read and best-known Buddhist scriptures.
summary: The Dhammapada contains 423 verses organized into 26 chapters, each dealing with a specific theme. The verses reflect core Buddhist teachings on mindfulness, ethical conduct, mental discipline, and the path to enlightenment.

verses.text: Mind precedes all mental states. Mind is their chief; they are all mind-wrought. If with an impure mind a person speaks or acts, suffering follows him like the wheel that follows the foot of the ox.
Mind precedes all mental states. Mind is their chief; they are all mind-wrought. If with a pure mind a person speaks or acts, happiness follows him like his never-departing shadow.
He abused me, he struck me, he overpowered me, he robbed me. Those who harbor such thoughts do not still their hatred.
He abused me, he struck me, he overpowered me, he robbed me. Those who do not harbor such thoughts still their hatred.
Hatred is never appeased by hatred in this world. By non-hatred alone is hatred appeased. This is an eternal law.
Those who think they can defeat others, and those who think they can be defeated—both are wrong. Both the victor and the vanquished experience suffering.
By oneself is evil done; by oneself is one defiled. By oneself is evil left undone; by oneself is one purified. Purity and impurity depend on oneself; no one can purify another.
Do not neglect your own welfare for the welfare of others, however great. Know well what is your welfare and be intent on it.
Control yourself, not others. The well-controlled person enters the untroubled land.
One should first establish oneself in what is proper; then only should one instruct others. Thus the wise person will not be reproached.
Earnestness is the path of immortality, negligence the path of death. Those who are earnest do not die; those who are negligent are already dead.
Understanding this, the wise person who is earnest rejoices in earnestness and delights in it.
The wise and earnest person, through zeal, meditation, and self-discipline, achieves the island that no flood can overwhelm.
The mind is hard to restrain, swift it flies wherever it will. To control the mind is good; a well-controlled mind brings happiness.
The mind is hard to see, subtle it flies wherever it will. The wise person guards the mind, which brings happiness.
Whatever an enemy may do to an enemy, or a hater to a hater, a wrongly directed mind will do us greater mischief.
Neither mother, nor father, nor any other relative can do as much good as a well-directed mind.

What the mind wishes, what the mind resolves, what the mind intends, and with the mind the deed is done. If one speaks or acts with a corrupt mind, suffering follows, as the wheel follows the hoof of the draught-ox.
Who shall conquer this world and the world of death, and the world of the gods? Who shall discover the shining Path of truth, as the expert shall discover the fragrant flower?
As on a heap of rubbish cast upon the highway, a lily may grow, sweet-smelling and delightful, so among blind followers of the Dhamma shines the disciple of the awakened Sage.
Long is the night to the sleepless; long is the mile to the weary; long is the way of the transmigration to the foolish who are ignorant of the true path.
If one holds oneself dear, one should protect oneself well. During each of the three watches of the night, the wise person should remain alert.
If one sees a wise person who points out faults and blames, one should follow such a wise person as one would a guide to hidden treasure.
Let one admonish, instruct, and dissuade one from wrong; the well-loved are those who displease the displeased.
Let one associate with the good; from the association with the good, the good comes. From the association with the good, no evil comes.
Better than a thousand empty words is one word of sense that brings peace.
Better is one word of sense that brings peace than a thousand empty words.
If one recites a single verse of Dhamma with understanding, it is better than reciting a hundred verses that are meaningless.
He who holds anger back as a charioteer holds back a chariot, him I call a real charioteer; other charioteers only hold the reins.
Conquer anger by non-anger; conquer the wicked by goodness; conquer the miser by generosity; conquer the liar by truth.
One should speak the truth, not yield to anger, and give to one who asks, even if it be little. By these three steps one goes near the gods.
One who is patient, gentle, and without arrogance, who is free from physical injury, self-controlled, and excellent—that person is worthy of robes.
Through earnestness, meditation, self-discipline, and self-mastery, the wise person makes for himself an island that no flood can overwhelm.
If you see the one who points out faults and blames, follow such a wise person as one would a revealer of treasures. It is better, not worse, to follow such a one.
The wise one admonishes, instructs, and dissuades from wrong; the pleasing are displeased to the displeasing.
He who instructs others, teaches and forbids, is loved by the good, disliked by the wicked.
The Tathagata, the fully enlightened One, is free from all fetters. He has attained the highest wisdom. He has reached the top, like the swan on the wing.
Happiness comes from serving others; happiness comes from giving to others; happiness comes from doing good deeds.
The father and mother are happy when the son is well-behaved. So is the sage when he sees his disciples grow.
If one holds oneself dear, one should protect oneself well. During each of the three watches of the night, the wise person should remain alert.
First, establish oneself in what is proper, then instruct others. Thus the wise will not be reproached.
If one should love oneself well, one should protect oneself well. During each of the three watches, the wise person should remain alert.
He who holds anger back as a charioteer holds back a chariot, him I call a real charioteer; other charioteers only hold the reins.
Conquer anger by non-anger; conquer the wicked by goodness; conquer the miser by generosity; conquer the liar by truth.
The best of paths is the Eightfold Path; the best of truths is the Four Noble Truths; the best of mental states is detachment; the best of men is the one with eyes to see.
This is the only path, there is no other that leads to the purification of the mind. Follow this path and conquer Mara.
Following this path, you will make an end of suffering. I have taught you the path for the dissolution of sorrow.
You must yourselves strive. The Buddhas show only the way. Those who practice meditation and are saved from the fetters of death are freed.
By what attachment is one hindered? By what is one freed from attachment? Give up desire and anger, and be freed from the fetters of attachment.
When one has abandoned desire and anger, the fetters of attachment are destroyed. Those who overcome pride and conceit, freed from all fetters, never sorrow.
Not in the sky, not in the midst of the ocean, nor in a mountain cave, is one free from the consequences of evil deeds.
Not in the sky, not in the midst of the ocean, nor in a mountain cave, is one free from suffering, except in the shelter of the Noble Ones.
Not by tonsure is one a Brahmin. If one is free from anger and fault, tranquil, self-controlled, and pure—one is a Brahmin.
By what should one be called a Brahmin? By what should one be called a monk? By what should one be called a renouncer?
He who has cut off all ill will and enmity, who is freed from anger, who is modest, who is free from attachment—that one I call a Brahmin.
He who has subdued himself, who is self-controlled, who has mastered himself, who has attained the highest self-mastery—that one I call a Brahmin.
He whose senses are tamed, like horses well-trained by a charioteer; he who has discarded pride, is free from impurities—that one I call a sage.
Such a sage, like a lake, is deep, difficult to fathom, and pure. Upon hearing the Dhamma, one becomes serene.
He who destroys anger by non-anger, the wicked by good, the miser by generosity, the liar by truth—that one I call a sage.
One who speaks truth, who does not yield to anger, who gives even when asked for little—that one I call a sage.
He who has cut off affection for son and cattle, who has renounced all sensual pleasures—who is free from the conceit 'I am'—him I call a true Brahmin.
He who has cut off all fetters, who is free from anger, who has transcended entanglements, who has uprooted attachment—that one I call a true Brahmin.
He who has discarded anger and conceit, who has overcome all fetters, who has uprooted attachment, who is free from the conceit 'I am'—that one I call a true Brahmin.
He who has no anger, who is devout, who is pure, who is free from passion, who has conquered himself, who is worthy of offerings—that one I call a true Brahmin.
He who has subdued himself, who is self-controlled, who has mastered himself, who has attained the highest self-mastery—that one I call a true Brahmin.
He who is not angry, who is devout, who is pure, who is free from passion, who is free from pride—that one I call a true Brahmin.
The mind is hard to restrain, swift it flies wherever it likes: to control the mind is good; a controlled mind brings happiness.
The mind is very hard to see, subtle it flies wherever it likes: the sage guards the mind; a guarded mind brings happiness.
The mind is volatile; it flies and dallies wherever it likes: a tamed mind brings happiness.
Whatever an enemy may do to an enemy, or a hater to a hater, a wrongly directed mind will do greater harm.
There is no fires like passion, no snare like hatred, no entanglement like delusion, no river like craving.
The mind is the forerunner of all actions. All deeds are led by mind, created by mind. If one speaks or acts with a corrupt mind, suffering follows, as the wheel follows the hoof of the ox.
Mind is the forerunner of all actions. All deeds are led by mind, created by mind. If one speaks or acts with a serene mind, happiness follows, as a shadow that never departs.
The fragrance of virtue travels against the wind and pervades all directions. The fragrance of virtue pervades heaven and earth.
The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind; but the fragrance of virtue travels against the wind; the good person pervades every direction.
As on a heap of refuse thrown upon the highway, a lily may grow, sweet-smelling and delightful, so among blind, erring mortals, the disciple of the truly awakened one shines with wisdom.
If many years a person live in evil, but overcomes evil, that person illuminates the world, as the moon freed from clouds.
If one man conquer in battle a thousand times a thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors.
He who lives only for pleasures, without self-control and mindfulness, wasting his opportunities, that idle person envies the strenuous.
Do not follow the low path, do not live in negligence, do not follow false doctrine, do not be attached to the world.
There is no disease like craving, no pain like mental formation, no happiness like the Dhamma, no suffering greater than the aggregates.
He who holds nothing dear and nothing hateful, he who has ceased former hatred and is not attached to new ones—he attains freedom.
He who loves nothing, hates nothing; there is no fetter for him whose passion is extinct. He who is free from desire and craving finds peace.
He who loves nothing, hates nothing; there is no fetter for him whose passion is extinct. He who is free from greed finds peace.
If you love a son, you will be troubled by the world. If you love nothing, you will have no trouble.
When lust is conquered, the fetters are broken, all sufferings cease, all chains fall away.
He who holds anger back, as one would restrain a chariot, is called a real driver; others merely hold the reins.
A true Brahmin who conquers anger is free from arrogance, sloth, and deceit. He is near the state of Nirvana.
Overcome anger by non-anger, evil by good, stinginess by generosity, falsehood by truth.
Be truthful; do not yield to anger; give to those who ask; by these three virtues you go near the gods.
Soon this body will lie on the ground, cast away, devoid of consciousness, like a useless piece of wood.
A man who is given to wrong thinking, who has removed the splinter, who has mastered the truth, has no more fear of his surroundings.
He who gives himself to vanity and does not give himself to meditation, forgetting the real purpose and seeking that which is not the true path, he rejoices in the world of the senses.
All fear the staff; all fear death. Putting oneself in the place of others, one should neither kill nor cause to kill.
He who seeks happiness by inflicting suffering on others will not find happiness, for happiness is only found in non-violence.
None should ever harm another, nor should one desire harm from another. A person who desires happiness should not harm any being.
The righteous should avoid the company of the wicked. The righteous should avoid the company of the wicked.
If a person is easily stirred to anger, he looks wretched. His body suffers; his mind is disturbed.
Whatever a hater does to a hater, or an enemy to an enemy, a wrongly directed mind will do greater harm.
Youth is not lasting; it is but the time of a meeting of the senses. Seeing that this is so, a wise person should refrain from sense pleasures.
Youth passes, beauty passes, they are but the time of a meeting of the senses. Seeing that this is so, a wise person should refrain from sense pleasures.
He who is like a withered leaf, which has come from the tree of birth—his time has come, his life is spent. Seeing this, a wise person should refrain from sense pleasures.
He who is like a withered leaf, which has come from the tree of birth—his time has come, his life is spent. Seeing this, a wise person should restrain himself.
One is one's own refuge, who else could be the refuge? One who has mastered oneself is an island of refuge.
Well have you done what should be done. You are the master of your own mind. Exert yourself, for self-control is the way to heaven.
A person is not an aristocrat by birth but by deed. One who speaks truth, who acts rightly, who harms no one—that one is a true Brahmin.
The wise person exerts himself more than the idle. The wise person is the true refuge of himself.
If one conquers oneself, one is greater than those who conquer a thousand men in battle.
He who conquers oneself is the best of conquerors. One who masters oneself is always serene.
Walk as if you are kissing the earth with your feet. Be steadfast, devoted, firm in virtue, gentle in spirit, humble.
Let the wise person guard oneself as a trader guards a valuable cargo. Let the wise person abandon evil, as one discards straw for gold.
The evil done by oneself falls upon the fool as dust falls on a fresh wind.

chapters.title: Buddha Vagga (The Awakened One / The Buddha)

verses.text: He whose senses are under control, like well-trained horses, free from pride, free from defilement—he is held in high regard even by the gods.
Like an elephant in battle, enduring the arrow shot from a bow, I will endure abuse, for most people are undisciplined.
The awakened one has no interest in sense pleasures, and his joy is in the destruction of craving. He uproots attachment and cuts off the fetter of existence.
Let a person live with the righteous, let him associate with the good. By association with the good, the good become good.
He who lives with the good, and who delights in the teaching of the saints, gains wisdom, and his suffering ends.
If you associate with a foolish person, you will suffer all your life. association with a fool is painful, as with an enemy.
Follow the good, follow the wise. The wise and the good are happy even in death.
The wise person should practice virtue, concentration, and wisdom, as the wise person practices restraint in the world.
